questions.text: How many years of full-time professional experience do you have?
follow_ups: Which employers and roles make up those years?
Are these years continuous full-time, not internships or training?
What was your longest single tenure, and what did you do there?
positive_evidence: States a clear total of four or more years
Names real employers and role titles with approximate dates
Clarifies full-time employment versus internships or coursework
Briefly describes responsibilities relevant to integration or automation
Mentions longest tenure with scope or key duty
red_flags: Gives a vague total with no employers or dates
Counts internships, part-time, or training toward the total
Cannot recall roles, timelines, or responsibilities

rubric.below_bar: Does not reach four full-time years, or is vague about employment history, counts internships/training, or cannot provide employers/roles.
rubric.excellent: States four or more full-time years, names employers and roles with approximate dates, distinguishes full-time from internships, and summarizes relevant responsibilities or scope.
rubric.meets_bar: States total years and at least some employer/role context suggesting full-time work; details may be sparse but plausible.

questions.evaluation_hint: Confirms the knockout of four-plus full-time professional years with concrete, falsifiable employment details.

questions.text: How many years have you worked hands-on with Workato in production?
signal_values: At least 1 year hands-on experience with Workato
follow_ups: Name one production recipe or connector you personally owned end to end.
Roughly what volume or impact did it have, like runs per day or users affected?
Which systems did it integrate, and what key triggers or actions did you use?
positive_evidence: States one or more years hands-on in production
Names a real recipe or connector they owned, not just assisted
Mentions scale signals like runs per day, SLAs, or user impact
Names integrated systems or apps involved
References Workato concepts like triggers, actions, connections, jobs, or environments
red_flags: Describes only tutorials, POCs, or shadowing with no production
Cannot name a concrete recipe, connector, or integrated systems
Confuses Workato with another platform or uses only generic buzzwords

rubric.below_bar: Less than a year or only non-production exposure; cannot provide a specific owned example; mixes up platforms or stays at buzzword level.
rubric.excellent: Gives a precise tenure of at least one year, cites a production recipe/connector they owned with integrated systems, scale metrics, and concrete Workato features used.
rubric.meets_bar: States at least one year and provides one specific production example with some concrete detail (system names, features, or scale).

questions.evaluation_hint: Gate on at least one year of production Workato work; listen for owned assets and real production detail and scale.

questions.text: You’re building a Workato recipe that calls an AI to auto-triage IT tickets. How would you design the flow so the AI’s decision reliably routes the ticket?
follow_ups: How would you keep context across steps without leaking sensitive data?
What safeguards would you add to catch bad AI outputs before acting?
What’s your fallback if the model is down or low confidence?
positive_evidence: References specific Workato constructs like triggers, callable recipes, variables, and error handlers.
Plans structured model outputs (e.g., JSON) with schema validation before routing.
Uses confidence thresholds, human-in-the-loop approval, or rule checks to gate actions.
Designs idempotency, retries, and dead‑letter paths for failed or uncertain cases.
Addresses security: PII masking, secrets management, and least‑privilege connections.
red_flags: Lets the AI decide and act with no validation, fallback, or human review.
No mention of concrete Workato mechanics or routing logic.
Ignores security, privacy, or data leakage concerns.

rubric.below_bar: Hand-wavy “the AI routes it” answer with no validation, fallback, or concrete steps. No Workato specifics; ignores reliability and security.
rubric.excellent: Clearly outlines a Workato-centric design: trigger → call AI → parse structured output → validate against a schema → route. Explains thresholds, fallbacks (manual queue or rule-based), error paths, idempotency, and masking of sensitive data. Names concrete Workato features and why they are used.
rubric.meets_bar: Describes a workable flow from model output to routing with some validation and a basic fallback. Mentions at least one Workato mechanism and some error handling, but lacks depth on thresholds, idempotency, or data protection.

questions.evaluation_hint: Assess ability to design a safe, deterministic AI-driven workflow in Workato with validation, fallbacks, and security.

questions.text: You need an AI agent that plans multi‑step actions across apps. How would you design its action loop so tool use stays safe and auditable?
signal_values: AI engineering focused on agent-based AI systems
follow_ups: How would you constrain which tools the agent can call and with what parameters?
How would you persist the agent’s state and recover from partial failures?
What limits or timeouts would you impose on the loop?
positive_evidence: Describes a planner‑executor or ReAct‑style loop with step caps and timeouts.
Defines an allow‑listed tool registry with schemas, parameter validation, and policy checks.
Adds approvals or guardrails for high‑impact actions; sandboxes side effects and supports rollbacks.
Persists state and traces with correlation IDs in a store (e.g., DB/queue) for audit and recovery.
Addresses rate limits and partial failure handling with compensating actions or retries.
red_flags: Says the agent can call any API freely with no constraints or audit.
No plan for state persistence, observability, or rollback; purely aspirational buzzwords.

rubric.below_bar: Vague “let the agent figure it out” answer with no controls, no state, and no audit. Cannot explain how to keep actions safe.
rubric.excellent: Explains a concrete agent loop (plan→act→observe) with strict tool contracts, allow‑lists, validation, and human/policy gates. Covers state storage, correlation IDs, audit logs, failure recovery, and limits/timeouts. Justifies choices and risks.
rubric.meets_bar: Outlines a reasonable loop with some tool constraints and basic logging or approval. Mentions state or recovery at a high level but lacks specifics on schemas, limits, or auditability.

questions.evaluation_hint: Probe for real agent safety design: tool contracts, state, limits, audit, and recovery — not just LLM buzzwords.

questions.text: You’re building a custom connector to a REST API that enforces rate limits. How would you design around the limit to avoid dropped calls?
follow_ups: How would you page through large result sets?
What’s your retry and backoff approach on five‑x responses?
How would you ensure idempotency on retries?
positive_evidence: Handles HTTP 429 and Retry‑After headers; proposes client‑side throttling or token‑bucket control.
Uses Workato concurrency controls or job queues to shape traffic.
Explains cursor/next‑link pagination and avoids expensive deep offsets.
Describes exponential backoff with jitter and a circuit breaker for persistent failures.
Ensures idempotency with keys or conditional requests to avoid duplicates on retries.
red_flags: Says to “just retry” without backoff or respecting headers.
Ignores pagination strategy or uses naive offset only; no idempotency plan.

rubric.below_bar: Shallow answer that ignores headers, retries aggressively, or omits pagination and idempotency.
rubric.excellent: Details a rate‑aware design: respects 429/Retry‑After, implements client throttling, shapes concurrency, and uses cursor‑based pagination. Explains backoff with jitter, circuit breaker, and idempotency keys/conditional requests.
rubric.meets_bar: Mentions rate limits and retries with some backoff and a basic pagination plan. May miss concurrency shaping or idempotency details.

questions.evaluation_hint: Listen for concrete REST practices: respecting 429, pagination type, backoff with jitter, concurrency shaping, and idempotency.

questions.text: In whichever of Java, Python, or Ruby you’re strongest, how would you implement a robust JSON transform that validates and maps fields for an integration step?
signal_values: Proficiency in at least one programming language: Java, Python, or Ruby
follow_ups: How would you handle malformed or unexpected input?
How would you unit test this mapping logic?
How would you log enough detail without leaking sensitive data?
positive_evidence: Names one of the languages and appropriate libraries (e.g., Java Jackson/Gson; Python jsonschema/Pydantic; Ruby JSON::Schema), not tool‑specific.
Validates against a schema and handles types, nulls, and defaults before mapping.
Uses defensive parsing with try/catch and routes bad records to a dead letter or quarantine.
Describes unit tests with edge cases and golden samples; separates pure mapping from I/O.
Mentions structured logs with redaction or field‑level masking.
red_flags: Lists buzzwords without a concrete language choice or libraries.
Skips validation and tests; relies on prints and happy‑path parsing.

rubric.below_bar: Vague, language‑agnostic answer with no libraries, no validation, and no testing strategy.
rubric.excellent: Picks a valid language and cites suitable libraries. Walks validation (schema, types), defensive parsing, clear mapping, and error routing. Explains unit tests and logging with redaction. The approach is implementable and language‑appropriate.
rubric.meets_bar: Chooses a language and outlines mapping with some validation and basic tests. Missing depth on schema, error routing, or logging hygiene.

questions.evaluation_hint: Confirms real hands‑on depth in at least one of Java/Python/Ruby by describing concrete JSON validation, mapping, errors, and tests.

questions.text: A Workato recipe that syncs tickets is intermittently timing out in production. How would you investigate and improve its reliability?
signal_values: Monitor, troubleshoot, and optimize integration workflows for performance and reliability
follow_ups: Which logs, metrics, or dashboards would you check first?
If the external API is slow, what optimizations would you try?
How would you add alerts to catch this earlier next time?
positive_evidence: Starts with Workato job history, step timings, error details, and payload samples.
Checks and tunes concurrency, batch size, timeouts, and polling windows; considers queues.
Measures external API latency and uses bulk/async endpoints, caching, or partial syncs.
Implements retries with backoff, circuit breaker, and a dead‑letter queue for failures.
Adds monitoring and alerts on error rate, latency, and queue depth with thresholds.
red_flags: Suggests rerunning jobs or blaming the network without systematic debugging.
No mention of observability, concrete levers, or optimizations tied to findings.

rubric.below_bar: Unstructured guesswork with no concrete Workato signals or tuning levers; hopes retries alone will fix it.
rubric.excellent: Presents a structured debug plan using Workato observability (jobs, timings, errors). Tunes concurrency/batching/timeouts, addresses slow externals with bulk/async or caching, adds resilient retries and DLQ, and defines alerts and SLOs.
rubric.meets_bar: Provides a reasonable checklist (check logs, adjust timeouts, add retries) and some performance tweaks, but lacks systematic measurement or clear alerting.

questions.evaluation_hint: Evaluate systematic troubleshooting and performance tuning of Workato recipes, including observability, tuning knobs, resiliency, and alerting.

questions.text: Tell me about a time you led an integration project and had to align cross-functional teams on requirements.
signal_values: Collaborate with cross-functional teams to gather requirements and deliver automation solutions
Document technical specifications, workflows, and best practices
Provide technical guidance and support to team members and stakeholders
follow_ups: What did you personally do to gather and clarify the requirements?
How did you handle a conflict or change request during the project?
What was the outcome or impact when it went live?
positive_evidence: Describes a specific project with involved teams and systems
Uses first person to explain actions like workshops, interviews, or writing user stories
Mentions artifacts such as specs, diagrams, or acceptance criteria
Explains handling of changes, trade-offs, or stakeholder conflict
States a measurable or concrete outcome after delivery
red_flags: Speaks only in "we" without personal actions
Gives a generic story with no teams, systems, or artifacts named
No clear result or learning from the situation

rubric.below_bar: Keeps it generic and team-focused with no personal actions, lacks specifics on requirements work, and provides no tangible outcome.
rubric.excellent: Provides a clear STAR story with specific teams and systems, first-person actions to gather/clarify requirements, documentation or artifacts, conflict/change handling, and a concrete outcome.
rubric.meets_bar: Gives a real example with some first-person actions and a general outcome; may be light on artifacts or metrics.

questions.evaluation_hint: Tests real cross-functional collaboration and ownership; reward first-person, concrete steps, artifacts, and outcomes.

signal_metadata.value: 4+ years total professional experience
At least 1 year hands-on experience with Workato
Designing and implementing AI-driven workflows
AI engineering focused on agent-based AI systems
Integration project implementation (end-to-end connectivity across enterprise applications)
API expertise: RESTful APIs
API expertise: SOAP/XML
Data formats/structures: JSON
Practical experience with automation technologies and middleware
Proficiency in at least one programming language: Java, Python, or Ruby
Sound understanding of RDBMS or NoSQL databases
Experience with other integration platforms (TIBCO, Dell Boomi, MuleSoft)
Familiarity with cloud technologies (iPaaS, SaaS applications, cloud infrastructure)
Experience integrating cloud business applications (Workday, NetSuite, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Marketo)
Knowledge of microservices and API-based integration architecture
Knowledge of event-driven architecture
Exposure to BPM or RPA-based process automation
Monitor, troubleshoot, and optimize integration workflows for performance and reliability
Collaborate with cross-functional teams to gather requirements and deliver automation solutions
Document technical specifications, workflows, and best practices
Provide technical guidance and support to team members and stakeholders
BTech/BE or higher in CS/AI/ML or related field
Relevant certifications in integration platforms or cloud technologies
Stay updated with industry trends in AI, automation, and integration
